questions.question: Explain the difference between procedural and declarative knowledge representation with an example of each.
keyPoints: Procedural encodes 'how to do' - algorithms, procedures, methods
Declarative encodes 'what is true' - facts, assertions, rules
Example of procedural: a recipe or sorting algorithm
Example of declarative: 'Paris is the capital of France' or logical facts

questions.question: What is the KR paradox? How does it impact knowledge representation choices in AI systems?
keyPoints: KR paradox describes inverse relationship between expressiveness and tractability
More expressive languages make reasoning computationally harder
Forces trade-offs in representation design
Practical systems often choose less expressive but tractable representations

questions.question: Describe the structure of a frame-based representation system. How does inheritance work in such systems?
keyPoints: Frames have slots (attributes) and fillers (values)
Organized in class hierarchy (superclass-subclass)
Inheritance allows properties to be passed down to subclasses
Values can be overridden or specialized in subclasses
Supports default values for reasoning with incomplete information

questions.question: What is ontological engineering? Why is it important in building AI knowledge bases?
keyPoints: Process of creating formal specifications of domain concepts
Defines concepts, relationships, and axioms
Enables knowledge sharing and reuse across systems
Reduces redundancy in knowledge acquisition
Creates shared vocabularies for interoperability

questions.question: Discuss the key differences between semantic networks and frame-based representations.
keyPoints: Semantic networks use graphs with nodes and labeled edges
Frames use structured objects with slots and values
Frames provide more organized, structured representation
Semantic networks are more flexible but less structured
Both support inheritance but frames handle it more explicitly

questions.question: Explain the fundamental trade-off between expressiveness and tractability in knowledge representation. How does this influence the design of representation languages and reasoning systems in AI? Provide examples to illustrate your answer.
keyPoints: Expressiveness: ability to represent complex real-world knowledge
Tractability: efficiency of reasoning algorithms
First-order logic is more expressive than propositional but harder to reason with
Horn clauses trade expressiveness for efficient inference
Practical systems often use restricted representations (e.g., description logic)
The choice depends on problem domain requirements
Mention specific examples like Prolog (Horn clauses) vs full first-order logic
Frame problem and computational complexity considerations

questions.question: Design a frame-based representation for a university domain. Include classes for Person, Student, Professor, Course, and Department. Show the slot structure and demonstrate how inheritance would work for an Undergraduate Student class. Also explain how default reasoning could be applied.
keyPoints: Define Person as root with slots: name, age, address
Student subclass adds: student_id, major, gpa, enrolled_courses
Professor subclass adds: employee_id, department, research_area, courses_taught
Course has: course_code, title, credits, instructor, prerequisites
Department has: name, location, faculty, courses_offered
Undergraduate Student inherits from Student, adds: year, honors_program
Default reasoning example: default year is 'freshman', default gpa is 0.0
Slot constraints: types, cardinality, facets

questions.question: What is a hybrid representation system? Discuss why hybrid approaches are often preferred in building practical AI systems. Design a simple hybrid system that combines logic-based and frame-based representations for a medical diagnosis domain.
keyPoints: Hybrid systems combine multiple representation formalisms
Benefits: leverage strengths, overcome individual limitations
Example combination: rules + frames + semantic networks
Medical diagnosis hybrid: frames for disease symptoms and patient data
Logic-based rules for diagnostic inference
Rules can reference frame structures
Supports both efficient lookup and logical deduction
Improves explainability and maintainability
